bytes.topic: ethers.js — providers, signers, contract interaction, event listening
bytes.title: ethers.js — Providers, Signers, and Contract Interaction Without the Guesswork
bytes.body: ethers.js separates read and write concerns cleanly: a Provider handles chain reads (balances, calls, logs), while a Signer wraps a private key or wallet and can send transactions. A BrowserProvider bridges MetaMask into ethers v6, replacing the v5 Web3Provider. To interact with a contract, you attach either a Provider (read-only) or Signer (read-write) — the same ContractFactory handles both. For event listening, contract.on() uses a typed filter; always call contract.off() or contract.removeAllListeners() to avoid memory leaks in SPAs. One sharp edge: provider.getLogs() and contract.queryFilter() are distinct — queryFilter decodes log data using the ABI, getLogs returns raw RPC results. In v6, BigInt replaces ethers.BigNumber everywhere, so arithmetic is native JS. When switching networks, recreate the provider — stale providers silently query the wrong chain. Use provider.getNetwork() to assert chain ID before any sensitive write.

bytes.topic: Wallet Connection — WalletConnect, MetaMask, Coinbase Wallet, wallet abstraction
bytes.title: ethers_js Wallet Connection — BrowserProvider, WalletConnect, and Signer Abstraction
bytes.body:
ethers.js v6 unifies wallet connections through `BrowserProvider`, which wraps any EIP-1193-compatible provider — MetaMask, Coinbase Wallet, or WalletConnect's `UniversalProvider`. The key insight: every wallet ultimately yields an `AbstractSigner`, so your app logic stays wallet-agnostic once you have one.

For MetaMask and Coinbase Wallet (injected), wrap `window.ethereum` directly: `new ethers.BrowserProvider(window.ethereum)`. For WalletConnect v2, initialize `UniversalProvider` from `@walletconnect/universal-provider`, then pass it to `BrowserProvider` identically — the interface is the same.

Call `provider.getSigner()` to get an `ethers.JsonRpcSigner`, which exposes `.sendTransaction()`, `.signMessage()`, and `.signTypedData()` uniformly. Avoid storing the provider long-term — re-request the signer per session to handle account changes. Listen to `provider.on('accountsChanged', ...)` and `provider.on('chainChanged', ...)` via the raw `window.ethereum` handle, since `BrowserProvider` doesn't re-emit these. This pattern lets you swap wallets — MetaMask today, WalletConnect tomorrow — without touching transaction logic.

bytes.topic: IPFS — content addressing, DHT, pinning, IPFS vs Filecoin, CIDs
bytes.title: ethers_js + IPFS — Reading CIDs from NFT Contracts and Resolving Off-Chain Metadata
bytes.body:
IPFS stores content by what it is, not where it is. A CID (Content Identifier) is a multihash of the data — change one byte, get a new CID. This makes on-chain NFT metadata tamper-evident: the `tokenURI()` return value in an ERC-721 contract is typically `ipfs://QmXoypiz...` (CIDv0) or `ipfs://bafy...` (CIDv1 base32).

When you call `contract.tokenURI(tokenId)` via ethers.js, you receive a raw `ipfs://` URI — not an HTTP URL. ethers.js has no built-in IPFS resolver; strip the prefix and query a gateway yourself. The DHT routes your request to peers holding that CID, but peers are not obligated to persist it. Pinning explicitly instructs a node — yours, Pinata, or web3.storage — to keep it alive.

IPFS vs Filecoin: IPFS is the content-addressing protocol and DHT routing layer; Filecoin adds cryptographic storage proofs (PoRep/PoSt) and token incentives so storage providers are paid to retain CIDs long-term. For production NFT projects, pin via Filecoin-backed services (NFT.Storage, w3up) rather than relying on public DHT availability.

The CID your contract stores on-chain is the immutable anchor — gateway URLs are ephemeral convenience wrappers around it.

bytes.topic: Reading On-chain Data — eth_call, getLogs, event filtering, batch requests
bytes.title: ethers_js — Reading On-chain Data Without Wasting RPC Calls
bytes.body: ethers_js gives you multiple read primitives, but picking the wrong one wastes RPC quota fast. `provider.call()` maps directly to `eth_call` — use it for arbitrary calldata without ABI overhead, or to simulate state at a specific block by passing `{ blockTag: 12345678 }`. For event history, `contract.queryFilter(filter, fromBlock, toBlock)` wraps `eth_getLogs` with automatic ABI decoding — you get typed `EventLog` objects back, not raw hex. Build precise filters with `contract.filters.Transfer(null, recipientAddress)` to index only the second topic. Avoid open-ended `getLogs` calls without a block range; most RPC providers cap at 10k results or 2k blocks. For batching, ethers v6 doesn't expose `eth_call` multicall natively, but you can pair `provider.send('eth_call', [...])` with a Multicall3 contract or use `Promise.all()` on `contract.foo.staticCall()` calls — these coalesce into a single HTTP request when using `JsonRpcProvider` with its internal batch queue. Always set `blockTag: 'finalized'` for data you'll act on to avoid reorg surprises.

bytes.topic: Writing Transactions — signing, gas estimation, nonce management, transaction monitoring
bytes.title: ethers.js Transaction Mastery — Signing, Gas, Nonces, and Monitoring
bytes.body: ethers.js gives you fine-grained control over every transaction lifecycle stage. When sending a transaction, `provider.getFeeData()` returns `maxFeePerGas` and `maxPriorityFeePerGas` for EIP-1559 chains — use these instead of hardcoding `gasPrice`. Call `provider.estimateGas(txRequest)` with your populated transaction object to get an accurate gas limit; add a 20% buffer for volatile contracts. Nonce management is critical under load: fetch once with `signer.getNonce('pending')` (not `'latest'`) to account for in-flight transactions, then manually increment across batched sends. Never rely on auto-nonce when firing multiple transactions in a tight loop — ethers will re-fetch and collide. After broadcasting via `signer.sendTransaction()`, you get a `TransactionResponse`. Call `.wait(confirmations)` to block until mined — it returns a `TransactionReceipt` with `status` (1 = success, 0 = reverted). For real-time monitoring without blocking, attach to `provider.once('transaction', hash, callback)` or poll `provider.getTransactionReceipt(hash)` until non-null. Always check `receipt.status` — a mined transaction can still be a revert.

bytes.topic: ENS — name resolution, avatar, records, reverse resolution
bytes.title: ethers_js ENS — Forward Resolution, Reverse Lookup, Avatars, and Text Records
bytes.body:
ENS resolution in ethers.js v6 splits into two paths. `provider.resolveName("vitalik.eth")` returns the checksummed address, while `provider.lookupAddress(address)` reverses it — but ethers internally validates that the forward record matches the reverse claim, returning `null` if they diverge. This is a security check you'd have to implement manually with raw contract calls.

For richer profile data, call `provider.getResolver(name)` to get an `EnsResolver` instance. Its `getText(key)` method fetches arbitrary ENSIP-5 text records — standard keys include `"url"`, `"email"`, `"com.twitter"`, `"org.telegram"`, and `"description"`. The `getAvatar()` method goes further: it resolves the avatar record through multiple layers — plain URL, IPFS CID via a public gateway, and ERC-721/ERC-1155 NFT ownership verification — returning a resolved URL or `null`. Unlike v5's manual resolver calls, v6 coalesces multiple `getText` calls on the same resolver into a single multicall RPC round trip where supported.

Always null-check the resolver — unregistered names return `null` from `getResolver`, and calling methods on it will throw.

bytes.topic: viem — TypeScript-first, lightweight, low-level, replacing ethers in modern stacks
bytes.title: ethers.js to viem — Why Modern Stacks Are Dropping ethers.js
bytes.body:
If you've built with ethers.js v5 or v6, you know the pain: a 116 kB bundle, a class-heavy OOP API, and TypeScript types that require constant casting. viem solves all three. It's tree-shakeable by default — import only `getBalance` and that's all you ship. Its API is function-based, not class-based, so there's no `new ethers.providers.JsonRpcProvider()` ceremony. Instead: `createPublicClient({ transport: http(rpcUrl) })`.

The real win is type safety. viem infers ABI types end-to-end — if your ABI says a function returns `uint256`, TypeScript knows the return type is `bigint` without any casting. ethers.js v6 improved this with `TypedContract`, but viem bakes it in natively with zero boilerplate.

Performance-wise, viem uses native `bigint` throughout (no `BigNumber` wrapper), and its bundle is ~35 kB vs ethers.js v6's ~80 kB. Migration is straightforward: `Provider` → `PublicClient`, `Signer` → `WalletClient`, `Contract` → `getContract`. Most ethers.js mental models transfer directly — the API surface just gets leaner and more explicit.

bytes.topic: IPFS Pinning — Pinata, web3.storage, NFT.Storage, self-hosted nodes
bytes.title: ethers.js + IPFS Pinning — Wiring Pinata, NFT.Storage, and Self-Hosted Nodes Into Your Mint Flow
bytes.body:
IPFS pinning services ensure your NFT metadata and assets stay permanently accessible — unpinned content gets garbage-collected by the network. In an ethers.js workflow, pinning sits upstream of your contract call: you pin first, receive a CID, then pass `ipfs://<CID>` as the `tokenURI` argument to your ERC-721 mint transaction.

**Pinata** is the most battle-tested option — POST JSON to `/pinning/pinJSONToIPFS` with a JWT and you get an `IpfsHash` back synchronously. **NFT.Storage** (now folded into Storacha/w3up) used CAR-file uploads via their SDK, offering free cold storage but slower retrieval. **web3.storage** follows the same w3up model.

**Self-hosted Kubo nodes** give you full control: pin via the HTTP API at `localhost:5001/api/v0/add`, but you're responsible for uptime and replication.

For production dApps, always store `ipfs://` URIs on-chain — never a gateway URL — so the metadata stays gateway-agnostic. Use Pinata's dedicated gateways (`gateway.pinata.cloud`) at read-time to avoid public gateway throttling when your ethers.js frontend batch-resolves `tokenURI()` calls across hundreds of NFTs.

bytes.topic: Contract ABIs — what ABIs are, encoding/decoding, generating typed ABIs
bytes.title: ethers.js Contract ABIs — Encoding, Decoding, and Typed Interfaces
bytes.body:
An ABI (Application Binary Interface) is the contract between your JavaScript code and a deployed Solidity contract — it defines function signatures, parameter types, and return types in a JSON schema that ethers.js uses to encode calldata and decode responses.

When you call a contract function, ethers.js uses the ABI to ABI-encode arguments into the 4-byte function selector + padded hex payload. On return, it decodes the raw bytes back into typed JS values. This happens automatically via `Contract` instances.

The `Interface` class is the core primitive. You can use it standalone to encode/decode without a provider — useful for transaction introspection or building raw calldata. `interface.encodeFunctionData('transfer', [to, amount])` produces the hex payload; `interface.decodeFunctionResult('balanceOf', resultBytes)` unpacks the response.

For type safety, use `typechain` or `@typechain/ethers-v6` to generate TypeScript wrappers from your ABI JSON. This gives you fully-typed `Contract` instances where `contract.transfer(to, amount)` is statically checked at compile time — no more silent argument mismatches.

bytes.topic: wagmi — React hooks for Ethereum, connector system, TanStack Query integration
bytes.title: Ethers_js: Simplifying Ethereum Development with wagmi and TanStack Query
bytes.body: Using ethers_js with wagmi's React hooks and TanStack Query integration simplifies Ethereum development by providing a straightforward way to interact with the blockchain. The wagmi library offers a set of hooks for connecting to Ethereum, such as useAccount and useConnect, which can be used in conjunction with ethers_js to handle tasks like transaction creation and contract interaction. By leveraging TanStack Query's caching and data fetching capabilities, developers can optimize their application's performance and reduce the complexity of managing blockchain data.

bytes.topic: The Graph — subgraphs, indexing blockchain events, GraphQL queries, hosted vs decentralized
bytes.title: Ethers_js and The Graph: Subgraph Indexing for Blockchain Events
bytes.body: To index blockchain events using ethers_js, you'll need to interact with The Graph's subgraph protocol. First, create a subgraph by deploying a GraphQL schema to The Graph's node network. The subgraph will poll your blockchain for events and store them in a temporal database. Then, use the ethers_js providers and the @graphprotocol/json-RPC package to query the subgraph and retrieve event data. Note that you can either use a hosted subgraph (e.g., The Graph Studio) or deploy a decentralized subgraph (e.g., using the Graph Node).

bytes.topic: Multicall — batching contract reads, reducing RPC calls, gas savings

Event Indexing — why you can't rely on full node getLogs for production, subgraph vs custom indexer
Multicall — batching contract reads, reducing RPC calls, gas savings
bytes.title: ethers_js Multicall: Batch Contract Reads, Reduce RPC Calls, Save Gas
bytes.body: Multicall is a powerful feature in ethers_js that allows batching multiple contract reads into a single RPC call. This reduces the number of RPC calls, resulting in significant gas savings. To use Multicall, create an instance of the `Multicall` class and add contract calls using the `add` method. Finally, call `execute` to send the batched request. Here's an example:

bytes.topic: Event Indexing — why you can't rely on full node getLogs for production, subgraph vs custom indexer
bytes.title: ethers_js: Why You Can't Rely on Full Node getLogs for Production
bytes.body: When using ethers_js to monitor events on a blockchain, developers often rely on the `getLogs` method provided by full nodes. However, this approach has several drawbacks. Firstly, `getLogs` returns a list of logs that may not be ordered chronologically, making it difficult to accurately track events. Secondly, the method has a rate limit, which can lead to missed events if not managed properly. Lastly, relying on full nodes introduces latency and potential network issues. A more reliable approach is to use a custom indexer or a subgraph. With ethers_js, you can easily set up a custom indexer by leveraging the `Subscription` class and the `EventFilter` object. This allows you to directly interact with the Ethereum node and retrieve events in real-time, without relying on full nodes.

bytes.topic: RPC Management — rate limits, fallback providers, Alchemy vs Infura vs QuickNode
bytes.title: ethers_js RPC Management: Rate Limits, Fallback Providers, and More
bytes.body: When working with ethers_js, managing RPC connections is crucial to prevent rate limiting and ensure seamless interactions with blockchain nodes. ethers_js offers a convenient `providers` API to handle RPC connections. To mitigate rate limits, you can switch between providers using `providers.getNetwork().getRpcUrl()` or set a fallback provider using `providers.getFallback()`. Alchemy, Infura, and QuickNode are popular RPC providers, but be aware that each has its rate limits and usage policies. You can check the current provider's rate limit with `providers.getNetwork().getRpcUrl().rateLimit`.

bytes.topic: Testing with Tools — forking mainnet, Hardhat/Foundry test environment, mocking contracts
bytes.title: ethers_js — Testing with Forking and Mocking
bytes.body: When testing smart contracts with ethers_js, forking the mainnet or using a test environment like Hardhat or Foundry can be time-consuming. To speed up testing, ethers_js provides `Provider` and `Network` APIs for mocking contracts and environments. You can create a mocked provider with `ethers.provider.getSigner('0x...')` and `ethers.provider.getNetwork()` to return a custom network and signer. Alternatively, use `ethers.js-tests` to create a test environment with mocked contracts and providers.
